meta.note: 1377 STEM PhD/MSc programs; faculty rosters on 115 programs (1879 faculty). Unknown/Verify = not yet confirmed on an official page.

requirements.english: TOEFL iBT ≥90 (old scale) / IELTS 7.0; exempt with a US bachelor's degree (NYU qualifies)
requirements.admission_model_note: Direct admission to the program, then 9 units of CME 391 research rotations in year 1; permanent advisor must be aligned by summer of year 1, at which point the advisor assumes funding.

funding.note: Funded to department standard for the duration of the program via fellowship/RA/TA; auto-considered for Stanford Graduate Fellowship (3 yr).

requirements.contact_note: No official contact norm; the application's non-binding faculty-interest selection plus first-year rotations are the formal matching mechanism.

programs.data_currency: Data reflective of the posted Fall 2027 cycle pages, retrieved 2026-07-05. Fee/TOEFL are 2025-26 values — re-verify when the application opens Sept 2026.

faculty.name: Ching-Yao Lai
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Geophysics
faculty.summary: Fuses physics-informed deep learning with satellite observations to discover missing ice-sheet and ice-shelf physics: inferring ice-shelf viscosity and anisotropy from remote sensing, grain-size-sensitive composite rheology of ice flow, and meltwater-driven hydrofracture. In 2025 her group's multistage neural networks discovered new families of unstable singularities in classical fluid equations, extending earlier work on self-similar Euler blowup. The single strongest ICME-affiliated match for an ML-in-geosciences plus fluid-dynamics profile.

faculty.name: Oliver Fringer
faculty.title: Professor of Civil & Environmental Engineering and of Oceans
faculty.summary: Leads the Environmental Fluid Mechanics Lab and is principal developer of SUNTANS, a parallel unstructured-grid nonhydrostatic coastal ocean model. Recent work spans high-resolution simulation of internal-tide generation and energetics, estuarine hydrodynamics and sediment dynamics in San Francisco Bay, LES of stratified flows, and GPU acceleration of coastal solvers. Long record of advising ICME students on numerical methods for ocean simulation — a direct match for oceanic applications of CFD.

faculty.name: Eric Dunham
faculty.title: Professor of Geophysics
faculty.summary: Develops high-order, provably stable numerical methods (summation-by-parts finite differences) for coupled multiphysics PDE problems in geophysics: dynamic earthquake rupture with fault friction and fluid effects, injection-induced seismicity, volcanic conduit flow and eruption seismology, and tsunami/ocean-acoustic coupling. Recent work adds data assimilation and machine learning for monitoring fault and volcanic systems. Frequently advises ICME theses that are numerical-PDE method development driven by solid-earth and fluid applications.

faculty.name: Parviz Moin
faculty.title: Johnson Professor, Mechanical Engineering; Director, Center for Turbulence Research
faculty.summary: Founding director of the Center for Turbulence Research, the flagship turbulence-simulation center. Recent group work includes wall-modeled LES of full aircraft configurations, robust numerics for high-fidelity simulation, atomization and multiphase turbulence, shock/boundary-layer interaction, and increasingly ML-assisted subgrid and wall models. The CTR Summer Program draws researchers worldwide, including many in geophysical turbulence. Pioneered DNS and LES as predictive science.

faculty.name: Ali Mani
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Develops the Macroscopic Forcing Method, a systematic way to compute exact eddy-diffusivity operators and generalized closures from DNS data — a mathematically principled alternative to black-box ML closures, with recent papers on RANS closure operators and scalar transport. Also studies particle-laden and multiphase turbulence, flow instabilities in electrochemical systems, and numerical methods for stiff multiphysics. Highly relevant to anyone interested in data-driven turbulence modeling for geophysical flows.

faculty.name: Gianluca Iaccarino
faculty.title: Rodgers Professor; Chair, Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Leads Stanford's uncertainty-quantification group and formerly directed the DOE PSAAP-II exascale center. Research covers multi-fidelity and embedded UQ methods for turbulent-flow prediction, model-form uncertainty in RANS, particle-laden turbulent flows for solar-energy receivers, radiation-turbulence interaction, and exascale simulation workflows. Teaches ICME's UQ curriculum, making him a natural advisor for students combining UQ with computational fluid dynamics.

faculty.name: Eric Darve
faculty.title: Professor of Mechanical Engineering; Director of ICME
faculty.summary: ICME's current director. Research spans fast hierarchical-matrix and low-rank linear-algebra algorithms, parallel and GPU computing, and a large scientific-ML program: differentiable-physics frameworks (ADCME) for inverse problems in mechanics and geophysics, neural constitutive models, operator learning, and ML-accelerated PDE solvers, building on earlier foundational fast-multipole work. As director he shapes the program itself, and his scientific-ML-for-inverse-geophysics line intersects directly with geoscience applications.

faculty.name: Lexing Ying
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Applied mathematician known for fast algorithms — butterfly factorizations, oscillatory integral operators, sparsifying preconditioners — for high-frequency wave equations and integral equations. In the last few years he has analyzed neural-network approaches to PDEs and operator learning, contributed to sampling theory, and built a productive line in quantum algorithms for scientific computing including eigenvalue estimation and linear systems. Ideal for a theory- and algorithms-leaning thesis with scientific-ML relevance.

faculty.name: Stephen Boyd
faculty.title: Samsung Professor in the School of Engineering; Professor of Electrical Engineering
faculty.summary: Boyd leads work on convex optimization and its real-time and embedded applications. His group maintains the widely used CVXPY modeling language and fast first-order solvers such as OSQP, and recently released CVXPYgen for automatic solver code generation. Recent projects apply convex and stochastic optimization to quantitative finance, including multi-period portfolio construction and a modern take on Markowitz methods, as well as differentiable optimization layers, model predictive control, and problems in signal processing, machine learning, and engineering design.

faculty.name: Emmanuel Candes
faculty.title: Barnum-Simons Chair in Mathematics and Statistics; Professor of Statistics
faculty.summary: Candes is a leader in distribution-free uncertainty quantification. His recent work develops the theory and practice of conformal prediction, including methods robust to distribution shift, online settings, and conditional coverage guarantees, plus extensions such as prediction-powered inference and conformal risk control. He also advances the knockoffs framework for false-discovery-rate-controlled variable selection and studies statistical inference for fairness auditing and calibration of black-box predictive algorithms. He currently chairs Stanford's Statistics Department.

faculty.name: David Donoho
faculty.title: Anne T. and Robert M. Bass Professor in the Humanities and Sciences; Professor of Statistics
faculty.summary: Donoho, who coined compressed sensing, now focuses on the mathematical foundations of modern data analysis and deep learning. Recent papers characterize 'neural collapse,' a geometric regularity emerging in the final layers of well-trained deep classifiers, and study its prevalence and implications. He also develops principled spectral methods such as ScreeNOT for optimal singular-value thresholding and matrix denoising, and writes influentially on data science as a discipline and on frictionless reproducibility in computational research.

faculty.name: Charbel Farhat
faculty.title: Vivian Church Hoff Professor of Aircraft Structures; Professor of Aeronautics & Astronautics and Mechanical Engineering
faculty.summary: Farhat works in computational mechanics, high-performance computing, and model reduction. His recent research advances projection-based reduced-order models and hybrid physics/machine-learning surrogates for high-dimensional, parametric, nonlinear problems, enabling real-time and many-query simulations and digital twins for aeroelasticity, fluid-structure interaction, and computational fluid dynamics on deforming domains. He develops structure-preserving and hyper-reduction techniques, and pioneered scalable domain-decomposition (FETI) solvers. Applications span aircraft flutter, hypersonics, underwater implosion, and design optimization of complex engineered systems.

faculty.name: Ron Fedkiw
faculty.title: Professor of Computer Science
faculty.summary: Fedkiw designs numerical algorithms for physically based simulation of fluids, solids, and cloth, and increasingly blends these with machine learning. Recent work couples neural networks with physics-based solvers and differentiable simulation, including high-fidelity cloth and garment modeling for virtual try-on, robust volumetric and level-set methods, and learning-augmented approaches to computational physics and computer vision. His techniques, rooted in PhysBAM, underpin special-effects tools used across the film industry and inform data-driven modeling of natural phenomena.

faculty.name: Alison Marsden
faculty.title: Douglass M. and Nola Leishman Professor of Cardiovascular Diseases; Professor of Pediatrics and Bioengineering
faculty.summary: Marsden develops patient-specific computational models of cardiovascular blood flow to guide surgery and device design, especially for congenital and pediatric heart disease. Her lab maintains the open-source SimVascular pipeline and recently built machine-learning and reduced-order surrogates, including graph neural networks and AI accelerators, that approximate expensive 3D hemodynamic simulations in near real time. Additional recent work targets uncertainty quantification, differentiable anatomical modeling, and model-driven design to mitigate vein-graft failure after coronary bypass, bridging simulation and clinical decision-making.

faculty.name: Marco Pavone
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Aeronautics and Astronautics
faculty.summary: Pavone directs Stanford's Autonomous Systems Laboratory and leads autonomous-vehicle research at NVIDIA. His recent work integrates learning and control for safe autonomy, including foundation and generative models for driving-scenario generation and analysis, trajectory prediction, risk-sensitive planning, and provable safety certificates for learning-enabled systems. He develops methods for robust motion planning, closed-loop simulation, and verification of neural components, spanning self-driving cars, aerospace vehicles, and future mobility, and works on bringing large models into real-time robotic decision-making.

faculty.name: Aaron Sidford
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Management Science and Engineering and Computer Science
faculty.summary: Sidford designs provably fast algorithms at the interface of continuous optimization, graph theory, and numerical linear algebra. He co-authored the breakthrough almost-linear-time algorithm for maximum flow and minimum-cost flow, and has extended these ideas to incremental and dynamic graph problems, multi-commodity flow, and shortest paths. Other recent work establishes superlinear memory lower bounds for convex optimization, advances stochastic and accelerated first-order methods, and studies the complexity of solving Markov decision processes and reinforcement learning.

faculty.name: Madeleine Udell
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Management Science and Engineering
faculty.summary: Udell works on optimization and machine learning that detect and exploit hidden low-dimensional structure. Recent research develops randomized and sketching-based methods for large-scale and low-rank optimization, faster and more memory-efficient solvers, and generalized low-rank models that embed heterogeneous, messy, missing data into coherent representations for imputation and denoising. She also studies automated machine learning, hyperparameter optimization, and, increasingly, foundation models for tabular data, emphasizing interpretability, reliability, and practical deployment on real-world datasets.

faculty.name: Leonid Ryzhik
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Ryzhik studies partial differential equations and their applications to physics and biology. His recent work analyzes front propagation and long-time behavior in reaction-diffusion equations of Fisher-KPP type, including logarithmic delays and the influence of advection and noise, as well as enhanced mixing and diffusion by incompressible flows. He also works on wave propagation in random media, kinetic and stochastic PDEs, branching processes, and mathematical questions arising in the mean-field analysis of neural-network training dynamics.

faculty.name: Daniel Tartakovsky
faculty.title: Professor of Energy Science and Engineering
faculty.summary: Tartakovsky develops uncertainty quantification and physics-informed machine learning for complex multiphysics systems, with applications in subsurface flow, energy, and hydrology. Recent work introduces randomized physics-informed conditional Karhunen-Loeve methods (rPICKLE) for high-dimensional inverse problems, along with the method of distributions for propagating uncertainty through nonlinear PDEs. He also builds hybrid physics/data surrogates, learns linear representations for nonautonomous dynamics, and applies these tools to inverse modeling, reliability, and multifidelity training of predictive models across scientific and engineering domains.

faculty.name: Grant Rotskoff
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Rotskoff builds machine-learning tools for problems in statistical mechanics and computational chemistry. His recent work uses deep neural networks and stochastic optimal control to sample rare molecular trajectories and estimate large-deviation functions, develops generative and diffusion-style models for equilibrium and nonequilibrium configurations, and creates systematic, physically accurate coarse-graining schemes. He also contributes to the theory of neural-network training through mean-field and interacting-particle analyses, connecting optimization dynamics to nonequilibrium physics and free-energy estimation for many-body systems.

requirements.deadline_display: PAUSED for Fall 2026 — Fall 2027 TBD (historically Jan 3)
requirements.english: TOEFL/IELTS/DET for non-native speakers, no minimum; exempt if undergrad instruction was entirely in English (NYU qualifies)
requirements.admission_model_note: Direct program admission with no rotations; admission is not coupled to specific advisers — students choose an adviser (Princeton faculty or GFDL scientists) during year 1 while on a university fellowship.

funding.note: Minimum 5 years stipend + tuition; year 1 on a Science & Engineering fellowship, years 2-5 via CIMES, grants, or AI.

requirements.contact_note: Explicitly encouraged: 'contact potential advisers when preparing the application'; an Applicant Mentorship Program pairs applicants with current students.

faculty.name: Alistair Adcroft
faculty.title: Research Oceanographer (Senior Research Scholar), AOS/GFDL
faculty.summary: Co-architect of the MOM6 ocean model and lead of the ocean working group for GFDL's OM4/CM4/ESM4 coupled models; AGU 2021 Ocean Sciences Award. His flagship current activity is the Princeton/GFDL arm of M²LInES — co-led from NYU Courant by Laure Zanna — training machine-learned ocean and sea-ice subgrid parameterizations on data-assimilation increments and LES, and implementing ML mesoscale-eddy closures in operational-class models. Also known for iceberg modeling and finite-volume generalized-vertical-coordinate numerics. A built-in bridge for NYU applicants.

faculty.name: Robert Hallberg
faculty.title: Lecturer in AOS; GFDL Senior Scientist
faculty.summary: Lead developer of MOM6, the community ocean model used across NOAA for climate and operational forecasting. Research spans the numerics of generalized vertical coordinates via the vertical Lagrangian-remap method, energetically consistent mesoscale-eddy parameterizations (MEKE), dense overflow and boundary-layer mixing processes, and how ocean-model formulation shapes climate and sea-level projections. An ideal advisor for rigorous GFD plus numerical-methods training with direct impact on national modeling infrastructure.

faculty.name: Rong Zhang
faculty.title: Lecturer in AOS; GFDL Senior Scientist
faculty.summary: A leading researcher on the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation: developing observational AMOC 'fingerprints' for monitoring and prediction, and tracing AMOC impacts on Atlantic hurricane activity, Sahel and Indian monsoon rainfall, Arctic sea-ice decline, and the Northeast US shelf. Recent work addresses AMOC stability, the North Atlantic warming hole, and mechanisms of Atlantic multidecadal variability in coupled models. Strong fit for large-scale ocean-climate dynamics combining theory with coupled modeling.

faculty.name: Stephen Griffies
faculty.title: Lecturer in AOS (departed — retired from GFDL May 2025, now LOCEAN/IPSL Paris)
faculty.summary: World authority on ocean climate-model fundamentals and author of the standard textbook. Research covered regional and global sea-level change, ocean heat uptake, water-mass transformation frameworks, and mesoscale-eddy effects on tracer transport. His own site states he retired from GFDL in May 2025 after 29 years and took a position at LOCEAN/IPSL in Paris beginning February 2026 — the AOS page still listing him is stale. Do not target as a Fall 2027 advisor.

faculty.name: Stephan Fueglistaler
faculty.title: Professor of Geosciences; Director of AOS; Director of CIMES
faculty.summary: The central Princeton-side figure in AOS — program director, director of graduate studies, and CIMES director. His group studies the tropical tropopause layer and the processes setting stratospheric water vapor, including satellite-constrained estimates of its response to warming and the roles of QBO, ENSO, and Walker-circulation variability. A second major thread concerns tropical SST patterns and their control on radiative feedbacks and apparent climate sensitivity. Theory plus model-hierarchy orientation, well matched to a fluid-dynamics background.

faculty.name: Leo Donner
faculty.title: Lecturer in AOS; GFDL Physical Scientist
faculty.summary: Longtime GFDL scientist who develops the deep-convection and cloud physics in GFDL's atmosphere models (AM4 and successors). Recent work spans convective parameterization with explicit vertical velocities enabling aerosol activation physics, aerosol-cloud interaction uncertainty as a driver of spread in effective radiative forcing and climate sensitivity, and evaluation of simulated precipitation extremes. Students typically work at the interface of process-level cloud physics and global modeling with GFDL supercomputing.

faculty.name: Olga Sergienko
faculty.title: Research Glaciologist, AOS/GFDL
faculty.summary: Princeton-based theoretical glaciologist and GFDL's contact for ice-sheet dynamics. Recent work revisits marine ice-sheet instability theory — showing grounding-line flux and stability results are richer than classic MISI assumptions — plus damage-mechanics simulation of ice-shelf rift propagation, subglacial lakes, and coupling ice sheets into GFDL climate models. Mathematically oriented, using asymptotics and variational methods — an excellent match for a student trained in fluid dynamics.

faculty.name: Gabriel Vecchi
faculty.title: Knox Taylor Professor of Geosciences; Director, High Meadows Environmental Institute
faculty.summary: Former GFDL scientist, now Princeton's most prominent climate professor. His group studies hurricanes in a changing climate — frequency, intensification rates, and historical-record reconstruction — along with ENSO and Walker-circulation change, volcanic influences, and seasonal-to-decadal prediction using GFDL's high-resolution FLOR and SPEAR models, plus global rainfall and drought attribution. Runs a large, active group and regularly advises AOS and Geosciences students, with deep GFDL ties on both sides.

faculty.name: Raffaele Ferrari
faculty.title: Cecil & Ida Green Professor of Oceanography, MIT EAPS
faculty.summary: Studies the fluid dynamics of the ocean and its role in climate: turbulent mixing in the abyssal ocean and its control on the meridional overturning circulation, submesoscale dynamics, Southern Ocean upwelling, and the coupling of ocean physics to biogeochemistry and ice ages. Recent work combines theory, idealized simulation, and observations to rethink how the deep ocean overturns, with implications for heat and carbon uptake. A classic destination for students with strong fluids and applied-math training.

faculty.name: Amala Mahadevan
faculty.title: Senior Scientist, WHOI
faculty.summary: Physical oceanographer studying how submesoscale flows and upper-ocean fronts shape stratification, mixed-layer dynamics, and the delivery of nutrients and carbon: process-oriented modeling tightly coupled to field observations. Recent themes include frontal instabilities, the role of small-scale physics in phytoplankton blooms and export, and monsoon-region air-sea interaction. Suits students who want idealized fluid-dynamical process studies with direct observational grounding.

faculty.name: John Marshall
faculty.title: Cecil & Ida Green Professor of Oceanography, MIT EAPS
faculty.summary: Studies the general circulation of the ocean and its role in global climate, known for the MITgcm model and for idealized aquaplanet studies of coupled climate. Recent interests include Southern Ocean dynamics and sea-ice interaction, the ocean's role in setting climate sensitivity and ITCZ position, and geoengineering-relevant transport questions. His group blends theory, hierarchy modeling, and observations in the classic GFD tradition.

faculty.name: Mick Follows
faculty.title: Professor, MIT EAPS
faculty.summary: Leads the Darwin Project, building trait-based models of marine microbial communities embedded in ocean circulation models to understand how physics, chemistry, and ecology jointly set ocean productivity and carbon cycling. Recent work spans metabolic constraints on microbial growth, nutrient-cycle coupling, and using idealized theory to interpret global biogeochemical patterns. Good fit for students who want physics-grounded ecosystem and carbon-cycle modeling.

requirements.english: TOEFL (recommended min 100 iBT) or IELTS (recommended min 7.0) required for non-native English speakers; waived if a prior degree was earned where the language of instruction is English.
requirements.admission_model_note: Students are admitted to the AOSM program through GSAS (applying via Mathematics) rather than to a specific advisor. Upon entry each student is assigned an initial academic advisor and then identifies a research advisor by mutual agreement during the first fall, subject to Director of Graduate Studies approval and funding availability.

funding.note: All AOSM PhD students receive MacCracken Fellowships for up to five years, covering full tuition, registration fees, comprehensive NYU health insurance, and a nine-month living stipend; summer support is typically provided through faculty research grants.

requirements.contact_note: Admission is committee-based and advisor matching happens after enrollment, so pre-application faculty contact is not required, though reaching out can help gauge research fit.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: Oliver Bühler
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Bühler is a geophysical fluid dynamicist whose research centers on the interaction of waves and mean flows in the atmosphere and ocean. His work spans internal gravity waves, wave-vortex decomposition, wave turbulence, and statistical fluid dynamics, including methods for extracting internal-wave and vortical signals from sparse observations such as ship and float data. He contributes to fundamental applied mathematics and stochastic modeling of geophysical flows and authored a widely used textbook on waves and mean flows. Recent work develops spectral and data-analysis techniques for characterizing geophysical turbulence and wave fields.

faculty.name: Edwin Gerber
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Gerber studies large-scale atmospheric dynamics and climate using hierarchies of idealized numerical models. His research illuminates the internal variability of the atmosphere, the dynamics of jet streams and annular modes, and stratosphere-troposphere coupling, including how the stratospheric polar vortex and sudden warmings influence surface weather and predictability. He works on the atmospheric circulation response to climate change, gravity-wave driving of the middle atmosphere, and reduced models that connect theory with comprehensive simulations. Recent projects also apply machine learning and idealized frameworks to understand and predict circulation regimes.

faculty.name: David Holland
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Holland investigates the coupled dynamics of the ocean, atmosphere, and polar ice sheets, focusing on how warm ocean waters drive melting of Antarctic and Greenland glaciers and ice shelves. Combining field observations, remote sensing, and geophysical fluid dynamics modeling, he studies ice-ocean interactions near glacier grounding lines and their implications for future sea-level rise. He has led major field campaigns, including work at Thwaites Glacier, deploying instruments beneath ice and in fjords. His research connects small-scale ocean turbulence and melt processes to global climate and sea-level projections.

faculty.name: Zongyi Li
faculty.title: Incoming Assistant Professor (joint appointment, Center for Data Science and Mathematics)
faculty.summary: Li develops machine-learning methods for scientific computing and is best known for pioneering neural operators, including the Fourier Neural Operator, which learn mappings between function spaces to solve families of partial differential equations far faster than traditional solvers. His research targets fast, generalizable surrogate models for problems in fluid dynamics, weather and climate, and multiphysics simulation, blending deep learning with numerical analysis. As an incoming faculty member jointly appointed in data science and mathematics, he aims to accelerate scientific discovery through operator learning, differentiable simulation, and physics-informed machine learning.

faculty.name: Olivier Pauluis
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Pauluis studies the dynamics and thermodynamics of moist atmospheric convection and the general circulation. His research examines how phase changes of water, entropy production, and irreversible processes shape thunderstorms, hurricanes, monsoons, and the tropical atmosphere, treating the atmosphere as a thermodynamic engine. He develops theoretical frameworks such as the isentropic analysis of the atmospheric circulation and mean-flow decompositions, and uses high-resolution cloud-resolving and global simulations to connect convective-scale processes to climate. Recent work addresses moist convection, atmospheric energetics, and the representation of convection and precipitation in weather and climate models.

faculty.name: Sara Shamekh
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Shamekh researches atmospheric and oceanic turbulence, moist convection, and clouds, combining physical reasoning, statistics, and machine learning. Her work develops data-driven parameterizations and interpretable machine-learning models that capture how convective organization, clouds, and turbulent transport influence climate, aiming to improve their representation in coarse climate models. She studies convective aggregation and the coupling between small-scale processes and the large-scale circulation, and uses high-resolution simulations to learn physically meaningful reduced descriptions. Recent projects extract emergent variables and closures for subgrid processes using neural networks while preserving physical constraints.

faculty.name: K. Shafer Smith
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Smith is a physical oceanographer and geophysical fluid dynamicist who studies mesoscale and submesoscale ocean turbulence. His research addresses baroclinic instability, geostrophic turbulence, and the eddy fluxes that transport heat, momentum, and tracers, shaping ocean stratification and climate. He develops theory together with idealized and realistic numerical models to understand energy pathways, eddy mixing, and the parameterization of unresolved eddies in ocean and climate models. Recent work examines submesoscale dynamics, the vertical structure of ocean turbulence, and the interaction of eddies with the large-scale circulation and tracer transport.

faculty.name: Esteban G. Tabak
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Tabak is an applied mathematician working at the interface of fluid dynamics, data analysis, and machine learning. He is known for developing optimal-transport and density-estimation methods and for data-driven approaches to problems such as attribution, clustering, and conditional density estimation. His research applies these mathematical tools to weather prediction, climate variability, and the analysis of geophysical and physical datasets, alongside work on stratified and rotating fluid flows. Recent projects advance explainable machine learning, factor discovery, and simulation-based approaches to sampling and transport with applications across the sciences.

faculty.name: Laure Zanna
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Zanna studies ocean dynamics and its role in climate variability, predictability, and change. Her research combines theory, observations, and computational and machine-learning methods to understand ocean turbulence, heat and carbon uptake, and sea-level rise. She develops data-driven and hybrid parameterizations of unresolved ocean processes such as mesoscale eddies to improve climate models, and works on climate-model interpretability and uncertainty. She leads efforts, including the M2LInES project, to reduce climate-model biases by learning from data, bridging physical oceanography and scientific machine learning.

faculty.name: Yi Zhang
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Mathematics and Atmosphere/Ocean Science
faculty.summary: Zhang studies large-scale atmospheric dynamics and the interactions among circulation, thermodynamics, and radiation that shape climate. Her research examines tropical convection and precipitation, the structure and variability of the intertropical convergence zone and Hadley circulation, and how moisture and radiative processes couple to the atmospheric circulation. Using idealized models, cloud-resolving simulations, and theory, she investigates how the tropical atmosphere responds to warming and what controls the distribution of rainfall. Recent work explores convective organization, atmospheric energetics, and the dynamics of tropical rainbands in a changing climate.

faculty.name: Tapio Schneider
faculty.title: Theodore Y. Wu Professor of Environmental Science & Engineering
faculty.summary: Leads the Climate Modeling Alliance (CliMA), building a next-generation Earth system model that learns systematically from observations and high-resolution simulations: ML-calibrated turbulence and cloud parameterizations, uncertainty quantification via ensemble Kalman methods, and the dynamics of subtropical clouds and their role in climate sensitivity. Earlier foundational work on atmospheric macroturbulence and the general circulation. A flagship destination for ML-plus-climate-dynamics research.

faculty.name: Andrew Thompson
faculty.title: John S. & Sherry Chen Professor of Environmental Science & Engineering
faculty.summary: Physical oceanographer studying Southern Ocean dynamics: mesoscale eddies and their role in the overturning circulation, exchange across the Antarctic margins and beneath ice shelves, and the coupling of physics to biogeochemical cycles. His group pairs theory and idealized modeling with autonomous observing platforms — gliders and floats — deployed in polar oceans. Good fit for students combining GFD with a taste for observational campaigns.

faculty.name: Christian Frankenberg
faculty.title: Professor of Environmental Science & Engineering
faculty.summary: Uses satellite and airborne remote sensing to constrain the global carbon cycle: solar-induced chlorophyll fluorescence as a proxy for photosynthesis, CO2 and methane retrievals (OCO-2/3, TROPOMI), and detection of methane point sources. Combines radiative-transfer modeling, retrieval algorithm development, and flux inversions to link observations to process understanding. Suits students interested in the data/inverse-problem side of Earth observation.

faculty.name: Siddhartha Mishra
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics, Seminar for Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Works on numerical analysis of PDEs and the mathematical foundations of scientific machine learning: robust schemes for hyperbolic conservation laws and computational fluid dynamics, and in recent years leading results on neural operators, error estimates for physics-informed learning, and ML-based ensemble forecasting for weather and climate. His group bridges rigorous numerical analysis and modern deep learning for the physical sciences — a strong thesis destination for CSE students heading toward a computational PhD.

faculty.name: Patrick Jenny
faculty.title: Professor, Institute of Fluid Dynamics
faculty.summary: Leads research on computational multiphase and turbulent flows: multiscale finite-volume methods for flow in porous media, PDF methods for turbulent combustion, blood flow and microcirculation modeling, and rarefied-gas dynamics. Recent projects include hybrid stochastic-deterministic solvers and model-order reduction for complex flow physics. A core advisor for CSE students specializing in the fluid-dynamics track.

faculty.name: Nicolas Gruber
faculty.title: Professor of Environmental Physics
faculty.summary: Environmental physicist studying the global carbon cycle and ocean biogeochemistry: the ocean sink for anthropogenic CO2 and its variability, ocean acidification and marine heatwave/compound extreme events, deoxygenation, and high-resolution regional biogeochemical modeling. His group combines global synthesis of observations with eddy-resolving simulation. Relevant for CSE students who want climate-flavored applications of large-scale computation.

requirements.english: IELTS ≥6.5 / TOEFL ≥93 unless prior English-medium education — Verify
requirements.admission_model_note: Admission on academic background (physics/math heavy); research projects at IMAU chosen during the program.

funding.note: Statutory EU tuition ≈€2.5k/yr; non-EU ≈€21k/yr; Utrecht Excellence Scholarships available — Verify.

requirements.contact_note: Not required for admission; useful when choosing an IMAU research group for the thesis year.

programs.data_currency: MOCK entry for UI testing — values illustrative; verify against uu.nl before use.

faculty.name: Henk Dijkstra
faculty.title: Professor of Dynamical Oceanography, IMAU
faculty.summary: Applies dynamical-systems theory to climate: stability and tipping behavior of the Atlantic overturning circulation, transition probabilities computed with rare-event techniques, ENSO and multidecadal variability as bifurcation phenomena, and complex-network approaches to climate data. Author of standard texts on nonlinear climate dynamics. Recent work couples machine learning with dynamical-systems analysis of tipping points — a distinctive, theory-first take on climate physics.

faculty.name: Anna von der Heydt
faculty.title: Professor of Climate Dynamics, IMAU
faculty.summary: Studies climate sensitivity and variability across timescales, using paleoclimate records and model hierarchies to constrain how sensitivity depends on the background state, and analyzing tipping elements and their interactions in the Earth system. Coordinates European projects on safe operating spaces and tipping-point early warning. Combines conceptual models, GCM experiments, and data analysis — well suited to physics-trained students.

faculty.name: Roderik van de Wal
faculty.title: Professor of Sea Level Change & Ice Sheets, IMAU
faculty.summary: Works on ice sheets and sea-level change: surface mass balance of Greenland from long-running observational transects, ice-dynamical modeling across glacial cycles, and regional-to-global sea-level projections including high-end scenarios used in coastal planning. Contributing author roles in IPCC assessments. His group links cryospheric process understanding to societally relevant sea-level products, with openings for both modeling- and data-oriented theses.

requirements.english: IELTS ≥6.5 (standard Imperial requirement) unless prior English-medium degree — Verify
requirements.admission_model_note: MRes projects are supervisor-attached from the start; applicants indicate project/supervisor preferences at application.

funding.note: Departmental and CDT-linked studentships for some places; many students self-fund the 1-year MRes — Verify.

requirements.contact_note: Because projects are supervisor-attached, emailing potential supervisors before applying is expected practice.

programs.data_currency: MOCK entry for UI testing — programme structure and values illustrative; verify against imperial.ac.uk before use.

faculty.name: Spencer Sherwin
faculty.title: Professor of Computational Fluid Mechanics, Aeronautics
faculty.summary: Develops high-order spectral/hp element methods and leads the Nektar++ open-source framework used across academia and industry for high-fidelity flow simulation. Research spans transition and separation in complex geometries, vortex dynamics, stability analysis, and industrial LES for motorsport and aeronautics. Recent work couples high-order methods with data-driven stabilization and GPU implementation. A strong supervisor for numerics-heavy fluid dynamics research.

faculty.name: Luca Magri
faculty.title: Professor of Scientific Machine Learning, Aeronautics
faculty.summary: Works at the interface of machine learning and fluid mechanics: physics-constrained neural networks and reservoir computing for predicting chaotic flows, adjoint-based sensitivity and data assimilation for thermoacoustic instabilities in aeroengines, and uncertainty-aware digital twins of reacting flows. Recent projects develop stability-preserving ML surrogates and real-time flow reconstruction from sparse sensors. An exact match for students targeting ML-augmented fluid dynamics.

faculty.name: Maarten van Reeuwijk
faculty.title: Professor of Environmental Fluid Mechanics, Civil Engineering
faculty.summary: Studies environmental turbulence with high-resolution simulation: large-eddy and direct numerical simulation of urban boundary layers and city-scale heat and pollutant dispersion (uDALES), the physics of turbulent entrainment in plumes, jets, and gravity currents, and mixing in stratified flows. Recent projects connect urban microclimate simulation to building design and climate adaptation. Bridges engineering CFD and atmospheric boundary-layer science.

requirements.english: English B2/C1 certificate unless prior English-medium degree — Verify
requirements.admission_model_note: Committee admission on CS/math background; thesis supervision arranged later across the Tübingen AI ecosystem (university, MPI-IS, Tübingen AI Center).

funding.note: No tuition for EU; Baden-Württemberg charges non-EU ≈€1,500/semester plus ≈€180 semester fee; DAAD and Deutschlandstipendium available — Verify.

requirements.contact_note: Not part of admission; contact research groups when seeking a thesis or HiWi position.

programs.data_currency: MOCK entry for UI testing — values illustrative; verify against uni-tuebingen.de before use.

faculty.name: Philipp Hennig
faculty.title: Professor for the Methods of Machine Learning
faculty.summary: Founder of probabilistic numerics: treating numerical computation — linear algebra, optimization, ODE/PDE solving — as probabilistic inference, so simulations return calibrated uncertainty. Recent work includes probabilistic ODE filters and their application to dynamical-systems learning, Laplace approximations for deep networks, and computation-aware Gaussian processes. Co-author of the Probabilistic Numerics textbook. Directly relevant to anyone blending scientific computing with machine learning.

faculty.name: Jakob Macke
faculty.title: Professor of Machine Learning in Science
faculty.summary: Develops simulation-based (likelihood-free) inference: neural density estimators that turn mechanistic simulators into statistically rigorous tools for parameter inference, with the widely used sbi software package. Applications span neuroscience, physics, and increasingly geoscience-style inverse problems where simulators are expensive and likelihoods intractable. Recent work improves amortized inference, model criticism, and scalability of SBI. A natural fit for science-oriented ML students.

faculty.name: Ulrike von Luxburg
faculty.title: Professor of Theory of Machine Learning
faculty.summary: Works on the theoretical foundations of machine learning: statistical guarantees for clustering and graph-based methods, ordinal embeddings and comparison-based learning, and recently the theory and critique of explainable ML. Known for the classic spectral-clustering tutorial and for rigorous analyses of when popular ML heuristics can and cannot be trusted. Suits students who want mathematically grounded ML alongside applied coursework.

requirements.english: IELTS preferred (TOEFL iBT or Duolingo accepted), complete by Dec 31; waived with an undergraduate degree of 3+ years from an English-language university in an English-speaking country.
requirements.admission_model_note: A departmental graduate admissions committee admits students to the program, not to individual advisors; students choose a thesis advisor during the first year or two after exploring courses and seminars.

funding.note: All admitted students are offered support (fellowship/TA/RA covering tuition and stipend); there is no separate financial aid application. Guaranteed number of years not stated on the admissions page.

requirements.contact_note: Admissions questions go to the Math Graduate Office (gradofc@math.mit.edu); pre-application faculty contact is not part of the process.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: Jörn Dunkel
faculty.title: MathWorks Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dunkel leads MIT's physical applied mathematics effort on living and active systems, developing continuum models and data-driven methods for biology. Recent work spans bacterial biofilm growth, active turbulence and chiral/odd fluids, curvature-guided pattern formation in development, and learning interpretable dynamical models directly from microscopy and single-cell data. His group combines nonlinear PDEs, topology, and statistical inference to explain self-organization in biological matter, collaborates extensively with experimentalists, and supervises PhD students on projects ranging from morphogenesis to network and knot dynamics.

faculty.name: Steven G. Johnson
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Johnson works on high-performance scientific computing and mathematical methods for wave phenomena, and is known for co-creating FFTW and foundational computational photonics tools (MPB, Meep). Recent research centers on large-scale inverse design and topology optimization of photonic and electromagnetic devices, fast methods for integral equations, special-function computation, and rigorous physical bounds on light-matter interactions. He bridges numerical analysis and physics, is heavily involved in the Julia and open-source ecosystems, and advises students on problems combining optimization, discretization theory, and device design.

faculty.name: Philippe Rigollet
faculty.title: Cecil and Ida Green Distinguished Professor, Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Rigollet chairs MIT's Applied Mathematics Committee and works at the intersection of statistics, optimization, and machine learning theory. His recent research develops mathematical foundations for modern ML: statistical optimal transport, Wasserstein gradient flows, sampling viewed as optimization over probability measures, and a widely noted program analyzing transformers and self-attention as interacting particle systems that explains token clustering in deep architectures. Earlier contributions span high-dimensional statistics and aggregation. His students train in probability, analysis of learning dynamics, and applied statistics.

faculty.name: Laurent Demanet
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics; Professor of Earth, Atmospheric and Planetary Sciences
faculty.summary: Demanet directs MIT's Imaging and Computing Group, focusing on inverse problems for wave propagation with applications in seismic and geophysical imaging. His research combines applied harmonic analysis, fast algorithms, and increasingly machine learning: low-frequency extrapolation for full-waveform inversion, learned regularization and priors for imaging, and stability analysis of inverse scattering. Holding a joint appointment in Earth, Atmospheric and Planetary Sciences, he supervises students on problems spanning numerical analysis, signal processing, and ML-assisted inversion for the Earth sciences and beyond.

faculty.name: John Urschel
faculty.title: Class of 1956 Career Development Assistant Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Urschel works in matrix analysis, numerical linear algebra, and spectral graph theory. Recent papers address eigenvalue perturbation bounds, graph Laplacians and their use in partitioning and graph drawing, determinantal point processes, and theoretical foundations of widely used algorithms such as Lanczos-based eigensolvers and low-rank approximation schemes. He is also known for expository and historical writing on linear algebra and for sustained mathematics outreach and mentoring. His research gives PhD students problems blending rigorous analysis with algorithmic questions from scientific computing and data science.

faculty.name: Martin Bazant
faculty.title: E. G. Roos (1944) Professor of Chemical Engineering; Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Bazant develops mathematical theory and models for transport, reactions, and interfaces in electrochemical systems. His group formulated theories of diffuse-charge dynamics, induced-charge electro-osmosis, and nonequilibrium thermodynamics of ion intercalation that reshaped understanding of batteries and electrokinetics. Recent work spans lithium-ion battery modeling, electrodeposition and dendrite control, water desalination and purification, and physics-based machine learning for electrochemical data. Bridging applied mathematics and chemical engineering, he applies these tools to energy storage, sustainability, and medical physics problems.

faculty.name: John Bush
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Bush studies interfacial fluid dynamics, combining experiments, theory, and mathematics. He is a leader in hydrodynamic quantum analogs, showing that millimetric droplets walking on a vibrating fluid bath can mimic quantum behaviors such as quantized orbits, tunneling, and statistical interference through pilot-wave dynamics. His group also explores surface-tension-driven flows, biological locomotion at small scales, and the fluid mechanics of insects and plants. Recent work refines the theoretical foundations of walking-droplet systems and their connections to de Broglie's pilot-wave picture.

faculty.name: Alan Edelman
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Edelman works in numerical linear algebra, random matrix theory, and high-performance scientific computing. He is a co-creator of the Julia programming language, designed for fast, expressive technical computing, and leads the Julia Lab at MIT. His research connects the statistics of random matrix eigenvalues to applications across physics, statistics, and data science, and advances parallel and GPU computing, automatic differentiation, and algorithms for large-scale linear algebra. Recent efforts integrate machine learning with scientific computing through differentiable programming in Julia.

faculty.name: Anette Hosoi
faculty.title: Neil and Jane Pappalardo Professor of Mechanical Engineering; Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Hosoi works at the intersection of fluid dynamics, biomechanics, and bio-inspired design. Her group studies the mechanics of soft, deformable systems and how organisms move through and manipulate fluids, translating biological strategies into engineered devices and robots. She has analyzed snail locomotion, digging shellfish, and the physics of thin films and complex fluids. More recently she applies applied mathematics and data science to sports analytics, quantifying performance and strategy. She co-founded the MIT Sports Lab connecting researchers with teams and industry.

faculty.name: Bonnie Berger
faculty.title: Simons Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Berger designs algorithms that extract biological insight from massive, rapidly growing datasets. Her group pioneered compressive genomics, enabling analyses that scale sublinearly with exploding sequence databases, along with methods for network integration, structural bioinformatics, and single-cell analysis. She also develops privacy-preserving techniques for sharing genomic and medical data. Recent work applies machine learning to protein structure and function, drug discovery, and immunology, combining rigorous computer science with biology to address problems in genomics, medicine, and public health.

faculty.name: Gigliola Staffilani
faculty.title: Abby Rockefeller Mauzé Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Staffilani studies dispersive nonlinear partial differential equations using tools from harmonic analysis, probability, and analytic number theory. Her work establishes well-posedness, conservation, and long-time behavior for equations such as the nonlinear Schrodinger and Korteweg-de Vries equations, including landmark results on global well-posedness and energy transfer between frequencies. Recent research develops the mathematical theory of wave turbulence, deriving effective kinetic equations describing how energy cascades across scales in nonlinear dispersive systems, and applies probabilistic methods to random data and invariant measures.

faculty.name: Semyon Dyatlov
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dyatlov works in microlocal analysis, scattering theory, and quantum chaos, studying how classical dynamics governs solutions of wave and Schrodinger equations at high energy. With collaborators he introduced the fractal uncertainty principle, using it to prove spectral gaps for chaotic scattering and lower bounds on eigenfunction mass on hyperbolic surfaces. His research also treats Pollicott-Ruelle resonances of chaotic flows, decay of waves, and mathematical aspects of general relativity, linking dynamical systems, semiclassical analysis, and spectral theory.

faculty.name: Larry Guth
faculty.title: Claude E. Shannon (1940) Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Guth works in harmonic analysis, combinatorial and metric geometry, and their deep interconnections. He is known for introducing polynomial partitioning, a method that resolved longstanding incidence problems and drove major progress on restriction, Kakeya, and decoupling estimates in Fourier analysis. His work connects the geometry of tubes and lines, extremal combinatorics, and analytic inequalities, and he has contributed to systolic geometry and sharp forms of the restriction conjecture. Recent efforts push toward the Kakeya conjecture and refined Fourier-analytic estimates.

faculty.name: Tobias Holck Colding
faculty.title: Cecil and Ida Green Distinguished Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Colding is a geometric analyst working on minimal surfaces, mean curvature flow, and the analysis of geometric partial differential equations. With William Minicozzi he developed a comprehensive structure theory for embedded minimal surfaces and, later, foundational results on singularities and generic behavior of mean curvature flow. His work also spans harmonic functions on manifolds, Ricci curvature, and the geometry of spaces with lower curvature bounds. Recent research studies uniqueness of tangent flows, singularity models, and quantitative geometric estimates.

faculty.name: William Minicozzi
faculty.title: Singer Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Minicozzi is a geometric analyst renowned for his long collaboration with Tobias Colding on minimal surfaces and mean curvature flow. Together they proved deep results classifying embedded minimal surfaces and analyzing singularities of curvature flows, including work on generic mean curvature flow and uniqueness of tangent cones. His research combines partial differential equations, differential geometry, and analysis to understand how surfaces evolve and degenerate. Recent work examines singularity formation, arrival-time equations, and the quantitative behavior of geometric flows.

faculty.name: Bjorn Poonen
faculty.title: Distinguished Professor in Science; Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Poonen works in number theory and arithmetic geometry, focusing on rational and integral points on algebraic varieties. He has developed methods for determining solutions to Diophantine equations, advanced the study of the Brauer-Manin obstruction, and produced influential results on undecidability related to Hilbert's tenth problem over rings. His research spans elliptic curves, the arithmetic of curves and their Jacobians, and heuristics for ranks and Selmer groups. Recent work refines Chabauty-type methods and statistical models for arithmetic objects.

faculty.name: Wei Zhang
faculty.title: Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Zhang works in number theory, automorphic forms, and arithmetic geometry, studying deep relationships between L-functions and arithmetic. He has made major contributions to the Gan-Gross-Prasad conjectures, the arithmetic fundamental lemma, and generalizations of the Gross-Zagier formula relating special cycles to derivatives of L-functions. His research connects Shimura varieties, Rankin-Selberg periods, and trace-formula techniques. Recent work develops arithmetic relative trace formulas and higher analogues linking intersection numbers of algebraic cycles to central derivatives of automorphic L-functions.

faculty.name: Pavel Etingof
faculty.title: David W. Skinner Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Etingof works in representation theory, quantum groups, and mathematical physics. His research spans tensor categories, rational Cherednik algebras, symplectic reflection algebras, and the interplay between noncommutative algebra and integrable systems. He has contributed foundational results on quantization, Hecke algebras, and the structure and classification of fusion and tensor categories. His work connects algebra, geometry, and physics, and he actively mentors young researchers as chief research adviser of MIT-PRIMES. Recent efforts explore representation theory in positive characteristic and new categorical structures.

faculty.name: Alexander Postnikov
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Postnikov works in algebraic combinatorics and its links to geometry, representation theory, and physics. He is known for foundational work on total positivity and the positive Grassmannian, introducing plabic graphs and combinatorial parametrizations now central to cluster algebras and scattering amplitudes. His research also encompasses generalized permutohedra, Schubert calculus, symmetric functions, and the combinatorics of polytopes and root systems. Recent work continues to develop the combinatorics of positive geometries, networks, and their surprising connections to modern mathematical physics.

faculty.name: Scott Sheffield
faculty.title: Leighton Family Professor of Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sheffield is a probabilist studying random geometric structures arising in statistical physics and quantum field theory. He has been central to developing the theory of the Gaussian free field, Schramm-Loewner evolution, and Liouville quantum gravity, establishing rigorous connections between random surfaces, conformal field theory, and scaling limits of discrete models. His work links percolation, random growth, and imaginary geometry. Recent research constructs and analyzes Liouville quantum gravity as a random metric space and explores its deep relationship with random planar maps.

requirements.english: TOEFL iBT 90+ (or IELTS, recommended overall band 7.0); waived only with a completed degree from an institution where the medium of instruction is English.
requirements.admission_model_note: Students are admitted to the Division of Applied Mathematics as a whole, not to a particular professor; advisor selection happens after arrival during the first year of study.

funding.note: At least five years of guaranteed funding, including stipend, tuition, health services fee, and health insurance.

requirements.contact_note: The Division notes you may email a professor directly with specific research questions, but admission is to the division rather than to individual advisors.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: George Em Karniadakis
faculty.title: The Charles Pitts Robinson and John Palmer Barstow Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Karniadakis leads the CRUNCH group, a driving force in scientific machine learning: he co-introduced physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and the DeepONet operator-learning framework. Recent work develops neural operators for multiphysics and multiscale systems, uncertainty quantification for scientific ML, fractional and stochastic PDEs, and applications ranging from blood flow and biomedicine to materials and fluid mechanics. His group co-develops widely used open-source SciML tools and collaborates with national labs and industry. Students typically combine deep learning, numerical analysis, and concrete scientific applications.

faculty.name: Björn Sandstede
faculty.title: Alumni-Alumnae University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sandstede studies nonlinear dynamics and pattern formation, combining rigorous dynamical-systems theory with computation and, increasingly, data-driven methods. Recent projects include spiral waves and their instabilities, localized patterns and snaking bifurcations, stability of nonlinear waves, agent-based and network models in biology, and machine-learning approaches to model identification and parameter inference. He is deeply involved in graduate training and undergraduate research mentoring at Brown, and his group's work spans mathematical analysis, scientific computing, and applications from cell polarization to ecology and cardiac dynamics.

faculty.name: Chi-Wang Shu
faculty.title: Theodore B. Stowell University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Shu is a leading figure in high-order numerical methods for PDEs, particularly weighted essentially non-oscillatory (WENO) schemes and discontinuous Galerkin methods, which he continues to refine for stability, positivity, and bound preservation. Recent work addresses entropy-stable and structure-preserving discretizations for hyperbolic conservation laws, convection-dominated problems, and applications in computational fluid dynamics, astrophysics, and kinetic and traffic models. His methods underpin many production simulation codes worldwide, and his students receive deep training in numerical analysis and scientific computing.

faculty.name: Kavita Ramanan
faculty.title: Roland George Dwight Richardson University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Ramanan works in probability theory and stochastic processes: large deviations, interacting particle systems, mean-field and local weak convergence limits, and stochastic networks with reflecting dynamics. Recent research develops rigorous frameworks for sparse-graph limits of interacting systems, hydrodynamic limits for load-balancing networks, and high-dimensional probability with connections to statistical learning and data science applications. She leads significant mentoring and outreach initiatives and advises PhD students on problems combining deep probabilistic theory with models drawn from engineering, physics, and operations research.

faculty.name: Brendan Keith
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Keith develops numerical methods for PDEs and computational optimization, with recent emphasis on adaptive finite element methods, PDE-constrained optimization under uncertainty, and the proximal Galerkin method for variational inequalities and pointwise-constrained problems. Other active directions include machine-learning-augmented discretizations and scalable solvers developed with collaborators at national laboratories. As an early-career faculty member holding a named assistant professorship, he is actively building his group, and his projects blend functional analysis, optimization, and high-performance scientific computing.

faculty.name: Mark Ainsworth
faculty.title: Francis Wayland Professor of Applied Mathematics; Director of Graduate Studies
faculty.summary: Ainsworth is the Francis Wayland Professor and Director of Graduate Studies in Applied Mathematics. His research centers on the numerical approximation of partial differential equations, including finite element methods, a posteriori error estimation, adaptivity, and high-performance scientific computing. He works on wave propagation, spectral and high-order methods, and hierarchical multiscale modeling, holding a joint appointment at Oak Ridge National Laboratory. A SIAM Fellow, he bridges rigorous numerical analysis with large-scale computation for challenging engineering and physical applications.

faculty.name: Johnny Guzmán
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics; Division Associate Chair
faculty.summary: Guzmán is a Professor and Division Associate Chair whose research focuses on the numerical analysis of partial differential equations. He develops and analyzes finite element methods, including mixed, hybridizable, and discontinuous Galerkin schemes, as well as structure-preserving and divergence-free discretizations. His work addresses elliptic problems on nonsmooth domains, elasticity, and incompressible fluid mechanics, combining rigorous error analysis with practical scientific computing. He is known for constructing stable, high-accuracy finite element spaces that respect the underlying physics of the equations.

faculty.name: Jerome Darbon
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Darbon is a Professor of Applied Mathematics working at the interface of optimization, partial differential equations, and scientific computing. His research connects efficient algorithms with Hamilton-Jacobi equations arising in optimal control, differential games, and variational and Bayesian estimation. With Stanley Osher he developed methods for overcoming the curse of dimensionality in certain high-dimensional Hamilton-Jacobi problems, an award-winning contribution. He also studies fast optimization for imaging and links between control, neural networks, and real-time computation.

faculty.name: Paul Dupuis
faculty.title: IBM Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dupuis is the IBM Professor of Applied Mathematics and a leading probabilist. His research applies probability to the control of deterministic and stochastic systems, emphasizing large deviation theory, stochastic control, and numerical methods for simulating rare events. He co-developed the weak-convergence approach to large deviations and pioneered importance-sampling schemes grounded in stochastic control and viscosity solutions. His work spans queueing and stochastic networks, Monte Carlo methods, and accelerated sampling, bridging rigorous probability with practical computation.

faculty.name: Hongjie Dong
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Dong is a Professor of Applied Mathematics specializing in the analysis of partial differential equations. His research addresses regularity theory for nonlinear elliptic and parabolic equations, the Navier-Stokes and quasi-geostrophic equations, and problems arising in composite materials. He also studies probabilistic approaches to PDEs and rates of convergence for finite-difference approximations of elliptic and parabolic Bellman equations from stochastic control. Combining harmonic analysis with PDE techniques, his work delivers sharp estimates for equations with rough or discontinuous coefficients.

faculty.name: Yan Guo
faculty.title: L. Herbert Ballou University Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Guo is the L. Herbert Ballou University Professor of Applied Mathematics and an expert in partial differential equations from kinetic theory and mathematical physics. His research studies the Boltzmann equation for dilute gases, Landau and Vlasov-Maxwell systems in plasma physics, and Vlasov-Poisson models for stellar dynamics, often focusing on nonlinear stability of steady states. He also analyzes classical fluid models governed by Euler and Navier-Stokes equations and their derivation from kinetic descriptions, developing techniques for boundary effects and hydrodynamic limits.

faculty.name: Govind Menon
faculty.title: Professor of Applied Mathematics; Division Chair
faculty.summary: Menon is the Division Chair and a Professor of Applied Mathematics whose work sits at the interface of dynamical systems, probability, and mathematical physics. He studies the kinetics of phase transitions and coarsening, integrable systems, random matrix theory, and statistical theories of turbulence, including Burgers turbulence. Recent interests include the formation and propagation of disorder in physical models and numerical algorithms, self-assembly, and mathematical questions arising in deep learning. His research blends rigorous analysis with modeling and computation.

faculty.name: Carina Curto
faculty.title: Pablo J. Salame Goldman Sachs Professor of Computational Neuroscience and Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Curto is the Pablo J. Salame Goldman Sachs Professor of Computational Neuroscience and Professor of Applied Mathematics, affiliated with the Carney Institute for Brain Science. Trained in mathematical physics, she now works at the intersection of mathematics and theoretical neuroscience. Her research develops the theory and analysis of neural networks and neural codes using tools from applied algebra, topology, and geometry. She studies how recurrent circuits support learning, memory, and sequence generation in cortical and hippocampal systems, linking network structure to computation.

faculty.name: Mara Freilich
faculty.title: Thomas J. and Alice M. Tisch Assistant Professor of Earth, Environmental, and Planetary Sciences and Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Freilich is the Tisch Assistant Professor of Earth, Environmental, and Planetary Sciences and Applied Mathematics, and is affiliated with the Data Science Institute. She uses mathematical modeling, observations, and data analysis to study ocean and ecological dynamics in the climate system. Her research spans fluid dynamics of ocean fronts and eddies, submesoscale processes, phytoplankton and microbial ecology, and reaction-advection-diffusion and stochastic models. She connects physical oceanography with biological productivity and carbon cycling, and pursues community-engaged science for climate justice.

faculty.name: Matthew Harrison
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Harrison is an Associate Professor of Applied Mathematics working in statistics, machine learning, and computational neuroscience. His research develops methods for conditional inference and multiple hypothesis testing and for detecting patterns in multi-neuronal spiking data. Affiliated with the BrainGate project, he designs statistical learning and prediction algorithms for online neural control of brain-computer interfaces. His work, published in venues such as Nature Neuroscience and the Annals of Statistics, blends rigorous statistical theory with exploratory data analysis of complex neural recordings.

faculty.name: Stuart Geman
faculty.title: James Manning Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Geman is the James Manning Professor of Applied Mathematics and a member of Brown's Pattern Theory group. He has made influential contributions to computer vision, probability, statistics, machine learning, and computational neuroscience. With his brother Donald Geman he introduced the Gibbs sampler and gave the first convergence proof for simulated annealing, foundational tools in Bayesian computation. His research explores compositionality, reusability, and hierarchy as principles for efficient learning in biological and artificial systems, developing probabilistic models of vision and natural scenes.

faculty.name: Oanh Nguyen
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Nguyen is an Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics working in probability. Her research studies random structures and their phase transitions, including random polynomials, random graphs and matrices, Markov chains, and optimal transport. A central theme is universality, understanding when limiting behavior is independent of fine model details. She has analyzed survival and extinction of epidemics and the contact process on random graphs, and the distribution of roots of random polynomials, publishing in journals such as the Annals of Probability.

faculty.name: Youngtak Sohn
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Sohn is an Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics whose research lies in probability theory and its interactions with statistical physics, theoretical computer science, and statistics. He studies mean-field spin glasses, random constraint satisfaction problems, and the analysis of stochastic networks, together with mathematical foundations of deep learning. His work on multi-species and spherical spin glass models establishes variational formulas for the limiting free energy and characterizes replica symmetry breaking, connecting statistical mechanics with high-dimensional inference and optimization.

faculty.name: Yair Shenfeld
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics
faculty.summary: Shenfeld is an Assistant Professor of Applied Mathematics, affiliated with the Data Science Institute, working in high-dimensional probability and its links to analysis, geometry, and mathematical physics. His research spans optimal transport, stochastic analysis, convex geometry, functional inequalities, and renormalization-group methods. He studies extremal problems in convex geometry, displacement convexity, and the mathematical underpinnings of generative modeling. Trained at Princeton and MIT, he develops geometric and probabilistic tools that clarify concentration, transportation, and inequalities in high dimensions.

faculty.name: Hui Wang
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Applied Mathematics; Director of Undergraduate Studies
faculty.summary: Wang is an Associate Professor and Director of Undergraduate Studies in Applied Mathematics, working on probabilistic methods and their applications. His research covers stochastic optimization, mathematical finance, Monte Carlo simulation, large deviations, and stochastic networks. Earlier work produced explicit solutions for path-dependent options under double-exponential jump-diffusion models and convex-duality methods for portfolio optimization in incomplete markets. More recently he has developed fast simulation and game-theoretic importance-sampling schemes for estimating rare-event probabilities, and he co-authored a textbook on Monte Carlo methods in finance.

requirements.deadline_display: Jan 12, 2026 final; Dec 1, 2025 priority (prior cycle — verify)
requirements.english: Non-native speakers must meet UW Graduate School English proficiency (TOEFL iBT or IELTS; waived with a degree from an English-medium institution) — verify current score minima in the Graduate School policy.
requirements.admission_model_note: Incoming students are assigned two faculty mentors for readings to explore research directions; a thesis advisor must be determined by the end of the student's first summer quarter.

funding.note: PhD students are supported via TA/RA appointments and fellowships; departmental financial support is limited to five years after admission, with extensions by petition. Verify guarantee terms in the offer.

requirements.contact_note: Advisor matching happens after enrollment via the mentor system, so contacting faculty before applying is not required.
